reasons.code_quality: Clean, idiomatic Next.js App Router code. Proper use of NextRequest, clean destructuring of Supabase responses, explicit column selection instead of wildcard, and early-return error handling. Variable naming (jwt, authError, postsError) is clear and consistent. No unnecessary abstractions or verbosity.
reasons.completeness: Every requirement and acceptance criterion is met: file exists, exports GET only, reads Authorization header with Bearer prefix stripping, calls supabase.auth.getUser(jwt), uses shared client from @/lib/supabase, filters with .eq('user_id', user.id), returns Response.json in all branches, returns 401 for missing and invalid tokens, returns 500 with error string on Supabase failure, and adds no unauthorized handlers or dependencies.
reasons.edge_cases: Covers the main error paths well: missing token (null jwt), invalid/expired token (authError or !user), and Supabase query failure (postsError). The optional chaining on authHeader?.startsWith correctly handles a null header. One minor gap: if the Supabase client returns null data with no error (an edge case in some SDK versions), the response would be { posts: null } rather than { posts: [] }, but this is unlikely in practice with the Supabase JS v2 client for select queries.
reasons.security: No hardcoded credentials, token validated server-side via supabase.auth.getUser before any data access, user_id filter scopes results to the authenticated user preventing cross-user data leakage, and parameterized query methods are used throughout eliminating SQL injection risk. The shared anon-key client is used for the DB query rather than a user-scoped client (which would enforce RLS automatically), but this is correct per the task constraint of reusing the shared client, and the explicit .eq filter provides application-level scoping.

overall_notes: Near-complete submission with no meaningful gaps. The implementation is tight and follows the spec precisely without scope creep. The only deductions are a minor edge case around null vs empty array on posts data, and the inherent (but spec-required) limitation of using a shared anon-key client for the DB query instead of a user-scoped client that would leverage RLS.
